text:
A journalist who was in person to witness the 46-second fight between Algerian boxer Imane Khelif and Italian Angela Carini has revealed the "simple" reason why the former should not have been allowed to compete, saying the International Olympic Committee should be "ashamed".

An Australian journalist has slammed the International Olympic Committee for allowing an Algerian boxer to compete in the Pairs Games, despite failing a gender test last year.

Imane Khelif, 25, caused huge controversy across the world following her Round of 16 bout against Italian Angela Carini which ended in just 46 seconds.

There were tense scenes inside the ring on Thursday, with Khelif's first punch knocking Carini's chin strap loose and the second shot landing on her chin.

The Italian was left visibly bloodied and later told reporters she feared her nose had been broken, before the 25-year-old decided to pull out of the competition.

News Corp journalist Peter Badel - who was there to report on the 66kg women's division - said Khelif should not have been allowed to compete at the Paris Games.

The 25-year-old was in 2023 disqualified and banned from participating just hours before her gold medal match at the women's World Championships in New Delhi, as she did not meet the gender eligibility criteria of the International Boxing Association.

The tests found the Algerian competitor - as well as Taiwanese boxer Lin Yu-Ting - had the presence of XY chromosomes in their system, typically found in males.

The duo were barred from competing and accused by the association's president Umar Kremlev of trying to gain an upper hand over their opponents.

"The debate is whether Khelief is a genuine woman… biologically a woman and testing shows she’s got the XY chromosome, which I believe rules her out," Badel told the Matty & The Missile podcast, starring Matty Johns and James Magnussen.

The journalist revealed he spoke with a medical professional who claimed the test result was the equivalent of a "female being on steroids".

"Steroids wouldn’t be allowed at the Olympics and in that case I don’t believe Khelif should be fighting against women," Badel continued.

â€œI have never been hit so hard in my life. Itâ€™s up to the IOC to judge.â€Italyâ€™s Angela Carini after lasting just 46 seconds against Algeriaâ€™s intersex athlete Imane Khelif.Biological firestorm at Paris Olympics.pic.twitter.com/BQRTF9Dc3m

After Khelif was declared the winner, Carini dropped to her knees in tears, later telling the media she was "heartbroken" and that she had "never felt a punch like this".

“I got into the ring to fight. I didn’t give up, but one punch hurt too much and so I said enough. I’m going out with my head held high," the Italian boxer said.

“After the second punch, after years of experience, I felt a strong pain in the nose. I said 'enough', because I didn’t want (to continue). I couldn’t finish the fight after the punch to the nose. So it was better to put an end to it."

Badel accused the International Olympic Committee (IOC) of having blood on its hands for permitting the Algerian into the ring at the Olympics.

Ahead of the Paris Games, the IOC gave the green light for Khelif and Lin to compete because the pair had passports which stated they were female.

The IOC released a statement hours after the fight saying it was "saddened" by the abuse the two athletes (Khelif and Lin) are receiving.

"Every person has the right to practice sport without discrimination," it said.

The IOC also noted the decision by the IBA was a "sudden and arbitrary decision", insisting the women had passed all necessary tests before participating in the Games.

Badel said the IOC should be "ashamed" of itself.

He urged the sports organisation to decide how it would deal with the issue of gender before the next Olympics or boxing championships, adding it was too late to ban or pull Khelif or Lin - who is also in the Round of 16 in Paris - out of their event.

The journalist said the IOC had to keep it to the facts.

"...But the bottom line is we need to just simplify this process and say: If you have the Y chromosome, you’re not a woman. You are a man," Badel told the podcast.

"It is as simple as that. And the IOC have to look at it like that, assess it that way, do the relevant testing and if the biology shows that you have the Y chromosome you fight against men. You do not fight against women."

There is no indication Khelif is transgender.

The president of Gaynet Communications in Italy, Rosario Coco, told Italian news agency ANSA he was advised Khelif was intersex.

"In contrast to the reports that have been circulating, the Algerian athlete Imane Khelif is not a trans woman," Mr Coco said.

"From the information we have about her, she is an intersex person, who has always socialised as a woman and has a sporting history in women's competitions."

Khelif will face Hungarian Anna Luca Hamori on Sunday (local time) in the quarter-finals.

Hamori told media she is "not scared" of the Algerian boxer.

"I don't care about the press story and social media. If she or he is a man, it will be a bigger victory for me if I win," the 23-year-old said.

Badel told the Matty & The Missile podcast Khelif can be beaten.

"Look I believe she can. Her record is 35 (wins) and 9 (losses). She’s not unbeatable, she’s not Floyd Mayweather," he said, noting four defeats had been knockouts.

title: Journalist Peter Badel says boxer Imane Khelif should not be in the Paris Olympics after previously failing gender eligibility test | Sky News Australia
description: An Australian journalist has slammed the International Olympic Committee for allowing an Algerian boxer to compete in the Pairs Games, despite failing a gender test last year.
